title: What Explains Differences in Finance Research Productivity during the Pandemic?
abstract: Based on a survey of American Finance Association members, we analyze how demographics, time allocation, production mechanisms, and institutional factors affect research production during the pandemic. Consistent with the literature, research productivity falls more for women and faculty with young children. Independently, and novel, extra time spent on teaching (much more likely for women) negatively affects research productivity. Also novel, concerns about feedback, isolation, and health have large negative research effects, which disproportionately affect junior faculty and PhD students. Finally, faculty who express greater concerns about employers’ finances report larger negative research effects and more concerns about feedback, isolation, and health.

article.introduction:
Editor's Note: Only rarely doesThe Journal of Financepublish research that examines the academic finance profession rather than the financial economics of households, corporations, and markets. This article is such an exceptional case. The COVID-19 pandemic has disrupted the personal and professional lives of many members of our profession. As a consequence, members' home institutions now face many difficult questions about how research and teaching contributions should be evaluated in light of this disruption. The research reported in this article based on a survey of the members of the American Finance Association provides some much-needed facts on these issues. To facilitate a timely dissemination of these research results, this paper was given an expedited review process.
The COVID-19 pandemic has rapidly upendedmodern life, tested the infrastructure of higher education globally, and pushed professors and administrators to their productive limits. Although there is now hope that effective vaccines will bring an end to the current state of chaos, there still may be long-term consequences for higher education. In this paper, we explore three main channels that might have disparate impacts on the research productivity of faculty (and PhD students) and hence their long-term career outcomes, which primarily depend on successful research records. First, we consider disruptions to time allocations, since the pandemic resulted in sharp disruptions in the way that faculty spend their time and these disruptions are significantly correlated with faculty characteristics such as seniority, gender, and family structure. Second, we consider production mechanisms beyond time allocation that can affect research productivity, such as the inability to obtain feedback, feelings of isolation, or concerns about physical or mental health. These production mechanisms likely affect productivity per unit of time input and thus can contribute to reductions in research independent of time allocations. We expect these mechanisms to vary with faculty characteristics like seniority, gender, and family structure. Finally, we consider institutional factors, such as the perceived financial condition of the institution and tenure-clock extension policies, and examine their impact on research productivity. To preview our results, we find that all three factors (time, production, and institutional mechanisms) play important roles in explaining variation in the effect of the pandemic on research productivity. Our results raise concerns that the pandemic will have long-lasting and disparate effects on, and thus shift the representation of, various groups in the faculty population.
Our study builds exclusively on data from a voluntary survey that we sent to the membership of the American Finance Association (AFA) on October 26, 2020. We asked members to evaluate the impact of the pandemic on their own research productivity and on the time spent on research and related activities. Because individuals with university-related positions comprise 85.4% of the 1,440 responses, we focus the analysis on this group. Most of the survey responses are reported in Likert scales (from 1 to 5), which is accommodated by ordered logistic models. This empirical approach allows us to examine the impact of predetermined variables (gender and family structure), rank (senior, junior, student), and mechanisms (time, production, and institutional factors), holding other things constant, and to conduct formal analyses of potentially important interactions. Because the pandemic hit everyone by complete surprise, and because the regressors in our models represent mostly pre-existing characteristics, endogeneity should not be a major concern in interpreting the regression results.
During the pandemic, most faculty report a negative effect on research, but importantly for our analysis there is considerable variation in this effect. Specifically, while 78.1% of faculty respondents report a decrease in research productivity and 60% report spending less time on research, 14.5% of faculty report an increase in research productivity and 21.5% report spending more time on research. The variation in research effects relates to predetermined factors—family structure and gender. Research productivity of women and faculty with children, especially very young children (between 0 and 5 years old), is particularly negatively impacted by the pandemic. These two factors appear to work independently without a significant interaction effect, that is, the presence of young children affects research production during the pandemic to a similar extent for men and for women, but women are more negatively impacted holding family structure constant. The professional implications of these effects are important. Women, particularly senior women, are already an underrepresented group within the academic finance profession, and thus efforts to ameliorate the disparity in recent years could see a setback postpandemic. Also negatively impacted are junior faculty of both genders, as they are more likely to have young children and experience professional isolation. Because junior faculty are the group for whom current research productivity will have the greatest impact on future career outcomes, the impact of these distortions may have profound effects on research and on the profession as a whole.
Motivated by the variation in survey responses highlighted above, we examine the mechanisms that generate disparate effects on research productivity. The pandemic and the resulting lockdowns and social distancing requirements greatly affect how academics spend their time. Faculty need to devote more time to teaching, as they turn attention to adopting entirely new models of remote and hybrid teaching. Faculty with children may spend more time navigating school and day care closures or may have increased responsibilities if adult children return home. To shed light on the time allocation mechanisms driving the negative research effects, we asked respondents to report changes in time allocation to tasks other than research. The time allocation questions are particularly telling, given the hard budget constraint of 24 hours a day. As expected, we find that faculty with children spend more time on childcare and domestic chores while cutting back on time spent on research, leisure, and sleep. In contrast, time spent on teaching is unaffected by the presence of children because teaching is a task with fixed deadlines that require immediate attention.
What has not been documented before and probably is less expected is our finding that women spend more time teaching and less time on research after controlling for family structure. Although increased time spent on teaching should be expected for all faculty given the rapid transition to online instruction, the fact that women report a bigger increase in their teaching burden than men further exacerbates the existing gender gap in research productivity.1Given the substantial evidence of bias against women (especially junior women) in teaching evaluations (e.g., Rivera and Tilcsik (2019), Mengel, Sauermann, and Zolitz (2019)), the pressure to gain favorable teaching ratings—an important component of tenure and promotion considerations—may elicit more effort from women, compared to men, on teaching at the expense of time for research (e.g., Winslow (2010)). Moreover, female professors tend to experience more demands and special requests from students as students continue to perceive and expect female professors to be more nurturing than male professors (El-Alayli, Hansen-Brown, and Ceynar (2018)). The pandemic, and ensuing major transition in the mode of teaching, only amplified this gap, an important finding that has not be discovered in other surveys and research. Importantly, gender differences in the increased time spent teaching do not explain the gender effect that we observe in research productivity. In contrast, the productivity loss due to children is explained by more time being allocated to childcare, chores, and teaching, rather than potential unobserved heterogeneity between faculty with and without children.
Having documented the effects of the pandemic on time allocation, we study the impact of changes in time allocation on research productivity. Time spent on teaching, childcare, and chores are all important determinants of research productivity, with time spent on childcare and chores having a particularly strong negative effect on research. These two variables predictably and reassuringly absorb the effects of the predetermined family structure variables (but not gender).
In addition to the directional effects, the economic magnitudes of these effects are interesting, which prompt us to extract marginal effects from the estimated ordered logistic models. The magnitudes of the effects of childcare and chores turn out to be enormous. Holding all other covariates constant at sample means, senior (junior) faculty who report spending “much more” time on childcare are 54.7 (47.2) percentage points more likely to report “much less,” that is, the most significant negative effect on research productivity. Although this finding is consistent with mounting evidence from other fields that young children are associated with significant reductions in productivity (e.g., Krukowski, Jagsi, and Cardel (2021), Myers et al. (2020), Deryungina, Shurchkov, and Stearns (2021)), the magnitude that we uncover is staggering. Moreover, these marginal effects still understate the total expected effects on junior faculty because they are also more likely to have young children than senior faculty, as 37.7% of junior faculty report spending “much more” time on childcare versus 29.4% of senior faculty. We multiply the probability of exposure to the pandemic-related childcare burden by the marginal effect of the burden on research productivity to obtain what we call the “expected effect” for a member of a given group. Using this measure, we find that a junior faculty member has a 17.8 percentage point higher probability of reporting the most extreme negative research outcome as a result of “much more” time spent on childcare (47.2 percentage point marginal effect times the 37.7% probability of reporting “much more” childcare). In comparison, the expected effect of childcare on research productivity for senior faculty is 16.1 percentage points, which is less than that for junior faculty due to lower exposure to a childcare burden.
The marginal effect of teaching on research productivity is smaller but still sizable. For example, senior (junior) faculty who report spending “much more” time on teaching are 39.0 (26.5) percentage points more likely to report “much less,” the most significant negative effect on research productivity. Senior faculty are slightly more likely than junior faculty to report spending “much more” time on teaching (23.6% vs. 19.8%) and thus the expected effect of a severe teaching burden is much larger for senior faculty than junior faculty (9.2 vs. 5.2 percentage points).
Although time diverted from research is a major factor behind drops in research productivity, the pandemic effect could also manifest in other ways. Professional conferences and traditional opportunities to disseminate research and obtain feedback may no longer be feasible or may not be as effective in remote formats. Moreover, like the general public, faculty may feel isolated or overwhelmed by concerns about their own health and well-being. These mechanisms likely affect productivity even for fixed units of work time, and thus can contribute to reductions in productivity independent of time allocations. As a result, we refer to these mechanisms as production mechanisms.
To capture the effects of production mechanisms on research productivity, we study well-being (concerns about physical and mental health) and two characteristics essential to the research development process, namely feedback and isolation. Specifically, we ask faculty whether they have concerns about lack of feedback on research, about isolation, or about their physical or mental health, with all responses recorded on a five-point Likert scale. A value of 3 indicates no change in concerns and a higher value indicates greater concerns).
After controlling for time allocations, we find that concerns about feedback, isolation, and health have independent negative effects on research productivity. The economic magnitudes of these effects rival those of the time allocation variables and also vary by rank. For example, senior (junior) faculty who report “much more” concern about feedback are 49.9 (41.7) percentage points more likely to report the most significant negative effect on research productivity. Although the marginal effect looks similar between the two groups, junior faculty are much more likely than senior faculty to have the highest level of concern about a lack of feedback (38.9% vs. 22.6%). Hence, the expected effect of the highest feedback concerns among junior faculty is more than 40% greater than that among senior faculty (16.2 vs. 11.3 percentage points). The marginal effects of health concerns on research outcomes are also large, but somewhat less pronounced than those related to feedback concerns (46.9 and 30.3 percentage points for senior and junior faculty, respectively). Again, because junior faculty are much more likely than senior faculty to have these concerns (30.7% vs. 15.2%), the expected effect gap is larger for junior rather than senior faculty (9.3 vs. 7.1 percentage points). The marginal effects of isolation concerns are similar to those of feedback and health concerns. Prior evidence shows that mental health affects employment prospects and workplace productivity (Bubonya, Cobb-Clark, and Wooden (2017), Frijters, Johnston, and Shields (2014)) and that the associated costs are large in magnitude (Greenberg et al. (2015)). However, our study is the first to report direct links between research productivity in academia to pandemic-driven increases in concerns about isolation and physical/mental health.
When we examine PhD students separately from faculty, we discover that factors affecting students are different. Although students report a negative research effect, it is less dramatic than that reported by junior faculty, and the time that students spend on research has not changed as much. For students, time allocated to teaching, childcare, and chores remain important, but the marginal effects are small relative to faculty. Moreover, students are less likely than faculty to report the most extreme time burdens from teaching, childcare, or chores. As a result, the expected effects on students are less than 4% for all three time-allocation variables.
The pandemic imposes a unique toll on students mentally, however, as they report greater concerns about isolation and health than faculty. Like faculty, these well-being and social connective factors negatively affect students’ research productivity independent of the time allocations. The marginal effects of the most extreme concerns on the probability of a student reporting the most negative research productivity impact are 23.4, 25.0, and 33.7 percentage points for isolation, health, and feedback, respectively. But 41.9% of students express the most extreme concerns about health and 32.8% express the most extreme concern about isolation. At the same time, students are less likely to report concerns about feedback, perhaps because their advisors continue to provide feedback or they lack a finished research product at the stage for external feedback. Although we do not ask about overall mental health (we combine physical and mental health and then separately ask about feelings of isolation), our findings are consistent with mounting evidence of significant mental health challenges during the pandemic. For example, Chirikov et al. (2020) report that between May 2020 and July 2020, 39% of graduate students screened positive for anxiety and 32% screened positive for depression. In comparison, the analogous figures for 2019 were 26% and 15%, respectively.2To be sure, graduate students (and faculty) are generally able to enjoy a level of intellectual freedom and work flexibility that can allow for a healthier work-life balance in normal times compared to individuals in other professions. However, the survey reveals that, like the general population, those in academic life are vulnerable to isolation and concerns about health as a result of COVID-19, especially those earliest in the career trajectory. We find direct links between these factors and declines in research productivity.
Finally, we turn to factors driving variation in research production coming from institutional factors. We find that negative research effects are larger for faculty employed by institutions with more serious financial concerns. Teaching becomes more demanding as universities struggle to retain students, and support for research decreases with budgetary cuts and hiring freezes. We also find that faculty who are more concerned about the financial health of their employers express greater concerns about lack of feedback, social isolation, and health.
Institutions introduced a variety of policies in response to the pandemic. Of these various policy responses, we focus on tenure clock extensions, as they intended to compensate for loss of research time, loss of access to data or lab space, and additional time required to adapt to new teaching models. Tenure clock extensions also help address career concerns and support the mental health of untenured faculty. We find that junior faculty employed by institutions that have extended the tenure clock report a decrease in research productivity, presumably due to a relaxation of the deadline imposed by tenure review. This may have been the desired effect, as universities made efforts to accommodate faculty experiencing pandemic-related stress and disruptions. However, it is possible that the variation in tenure clock extensions has increased dispersion in productivity within cohorts, to the disadvantage of some junior faculty upon tenure reviews to the extent that such reviews are based on comparisons within cohorts. Moreover, these policies do not appear to help alleviate pandemic-related stress: junior faculty at institutions with tenure clock extensions are no less likely to report feelings of isolation, concerns related to timely feedback, or overall health concerns. We hope that our findings raise awareness among institutions, and motivate them to expand coverage of mental health support during this difficult time and to provide clearer guidance on where and how to seek help when facing mental health issues.
Our study contributes to multiple strands of literature and has direct policy implications. First and foremost, we contribute to a quickly emerging field that examines the impact of the pandemic on the life and productivity of academics, usually via survey methods. We echo recent evidence that women and women with children in the STEM fields (science, technology, engineering, and mathematics, which includes economics) have been particularly affected by COVID-19 (e.g., Cui, Ding, and Zhu (2021), Deryungina, Shurchkov, and Stearns (2021), Myers et al. (2020)). In STEM, Frederickson (2020), Sugimoto (2020), and Andersen et al. (2020) report significant declines in research by female authors relative to men since the onset of the pandemic. Amano-Patiño (2020) reports that female economists are vastly underrepresented in the new and flourishing area of COVID research. Our evidence is also consistent with the findings in Kruger, Maturana, and Nickerson (2020) that although the overall volume of working papers increased during the pandemic, a gender gap in the posting of papers online has continued during the pandemic. Although evidence on the more pronounced impact of the pandemic on research for women and faculty with young children is growing, we still know little about the mechanisms driving these differences, which is the focus of our study.
An important incremental contribution of this study relative to extant survey-based research lies in our examination of three channels that may contribute to research productivity changes: time allocation, physical and mental health concerns (production mechanisms), and institution-level effects (financial condition and tenure extensions). In particular, going beyond the effects of gender and children, we ask how these predetermined family structure variables relate to time allocation decisions during the pandemic. Our approach is in the spirit of Myers et al. (2020) but we examine changes in time allocated to a richer set of activities (including childcare, chores, leisure, and sleep) to help us understand the mechanisms that drive changes in time allocated to research. The time allocation measures that we examine are similar to those used in contemporaneous work by Deryungina, Shurchkov, and Stearns (2021), but Deryungina Shurchkov, and Stearns (2021) do not study the relationship between time allocation and changes in research productivity, which is the main variable of interest in this paper. Further, unlike both Myers et al. (2020) and Deryungina, Shurchkov, and Stearns (2021), we examine respondents’ concerns about their own physical or mental health, isolation, and ability to obtain timely feedback on their work, factors that likely impede the exchange of ideas and feedback that is crucial to the production of impactful research. Thus, we explore a rich set of factors that potentially impact research productivity during the pandemic. We also extend this literature by studying the effect of the university's financial condition and decision to extend tenure clocks on research productivity outcomes.
We additionally contribute to this new field by exploring variations across professional ranks. Our data include large samples of senior faculty, junior faculty, and PhD students, which allows us to uncover novel evidence on the differential effects of time allocation mechanisms (e.g., teaching, childcare, and chores), production mechanisms (e.g., concerns about feedback, health, isolation), and institutional responses, that highlight potentially important implications for junior scholars and students, who will shape the profession for years to come.
The varying effects of COVID-19 that we document have implications that extend beyond academics in finance and beyond the specific setting of COVID-19. Many of the professional responsibilities of finance faculty and the research criteria against which they are evaluated are shared across a wide range of disciplines, and lessons learned from COVID-19 may apply to other major negative shocks to academic production. If administrators are not deliberate in their policy responses to the pandemic, the sharp decline in research productivity reported by faculty with children and female faculty will have lasting impacts on careers and the diversity of individuals contributing to knowledge production. In particular, we caution against one-size-fits-all policies, such as a uniform tenure clock extension, echoing the findings of Antecol, Bedard, and Stearns (2018) on gender-neutral parental leaves in a new setting and providing empirical support for the sentiment expressed in the opinion piece by Malisch et al. (2020). Our findings support policy responses that account for the disparate effects of a common negative shock (Oleschuk (2020)), for example, via an opt-out tenure clock extension, more support for junior faculty, and nonessential service relief.
The paper proceeds as follows. SectionIdescribes the data and reports summary statistics. SectionIIpresents empirical results on the impact of the pandemic on research productivity and time allocation. SectionIIIexamines institutional responses. Finally, SectionIVconcludes.
article.I. Data and Summary Statistics:
We designed the survey with two goals: (i) to collect a large sample that is less likely to be affected by selection and (ii) to obtain a statistically powerful observation count that allows us to explore not only the effect of COVID-19 on individual researchers, but also the underlying mechanisms. Toward that aim, we designed a short survey (less than five minutes) with no tracking of location or information that could reveal location so as to reduce potential concerns from respondents. The AFA kindly agreed to implement the survey.
After obtaining requisite Institutional Review Board clearances and signing data use agreements, we sent an invitation to take the voluntary survey to 8,421 members of the AFA via email on October 26, 2020, with a deadline of November 4, 2020. Two reminders were sent, on October 31 and on November 4, 2020.3A total of 1,440 responses were received by the deadline, for a response rate of 17.5% after accounting for bounced email.4
Given the importance of research productivity to institutions and to individual career outcomes, the key information we solicited related to how research productivity and time allocated to research were affected by the pandemic. We also asked questions about changes in research support, specifically financial resources and feedback from colleagues. The survey was anonymous, and we did not collect IP addresses or other identifiers. However, the survey asked for demographic information on gender, professional role, tenure status (for faculty), household structure (spouse/partner, children), and continent location. The surveyed individuals were also asked about their institution's response to the pandemic such as extensions of tenure clocks and elimination/optionality of student evaluations of instruction. Finally, we asked how concerned survey respondents were about a number of emerging issues related to the pandemic, such as isolation, online teaching, in-person teaching, children's education, employer and personal finances, and personal health. The survey questions as well as summaries of the responses are reported in theInternet Appendix.
In the main analyses, we focus on the respondents with university-related positions who identified themselves as male or female. We delete 42 observations for which all responses to time allocations are blank or all responses to concerns/worries are blank. The resulting main sample consists of 1,008 university respondents: 731 faculty (402 senior faculty and 329 junior faculty) and 277 students. The geographic distribution of the final sample is 55% for the United States, 25% for Europe, and 20% for rest of the world. The focus on university positions is driven by the objectives of the study, the relatively low number of responses from nonuniversity researchers, and heterogeneity in career demands—nonuniversity researchers face different trade-offs than faculty, as they typically do not teach, and they do not have a tenure-track process. We do not consider the responses that omit gender information as they do not allow us to uncover potential disparate effects on female researchers.
When we compare the sample of faculty survey respondents to the top 100 business school faculty in Sherman and Tookes (2020), we find that women and junior faculty are overrepresented among survey respondents while senior men are underrepresented. We present comparative sample weights in TableIA.IIAin theInternet Appendix. Senior men account for 58.7% of the sample in Sherman and Tookes (2020) versus 40.1% in our sample. All other groups, particularly junior women, are overrepresented in our sample compared to theirs. This might be expected, given that the data in Sherman and Tookes (2020) are limited to the top 100 U.S. schools and were last updated in 2017. Indeed, relative to the full targeted audience of our survey, their sample could underestimate the presence of women and junior scholars. To examine the potential impact of over- and underweighting, we calculate a set of summary statistics in which we re-weight the survey descriptive statistics to account for the sample composition in Sherman and Tookes (2020).
TableIreports summary statistics for our sample, the reweighted sample, and the subsamples of senior faculty, junior faculty, and PhD students. The average response to the question “How did the COVID-19 pandemic affect your research productivity?” (Research_Productivity) is 1.94 for faculty, based on a Likert scale from 1 = significantly negative to 5 = significantly positive (where 3 is the neutral value). Junior faculty are the most negatively affected group: both senior faculty and students report a less negative effect than junior faculty (a difference of 0.35,t= 4.06, and 0.38,t= 4.29, respectively).5
The mean decline in self-reported research productivity that we observe seems more negative compared to findings in concurrent research by Kruger, Maturana, and Nickerson (2020), who report a 35% increase in new finance and economics working papers posted to SSRN during the second quarter of 2020 following the onset of the pandemic.6There are several potential explanations for why the average survey respondent reports adverse effects on research productivity while aggregate working paper postings increased. First, although many scholars report decreases in productivity, not all of them do. Indeed, 14.5% of our sample reports that the pandemic has had a “slightly positive” or “very positive” impact on their research productivity. In fact, the productivity surge documented in Kruger, Maturana, and Nickerson (2020), who restrict attention to the top 50 U.S. departments, was particularly large in the top 10 departments. They show that COVID-19 has steepened the productivity curve, where more productive researchers may become even more productive. The focus on the top 50 departments in Kruger, Maturana, and Nickerson (2020) places heavy weights on this group, while our survey was sent to faculty at practically all schools, both in the United States and around the world.
Second, the productivity surge documented in Kruger, Maturana, and Nickerson (2020) appears to be a one-time surge at the beginning of the pandemic when many researchers could use the spring and summer months (which normally would have coincided with a busy “conference season”) to complete existing projects. Figure 1 of Kruger, Maturana, and Nickerson (2020) shows that monthly SSRN uploads drop closer to normal levels by the fall of 2020, which coincides with the timing of our survey (October 2020). Consistent with this pattern, a simple tabulation of SSRN FEN (finance field) postings of new papers for the full year by scholars from all institutions, without filtering for top-50 or top-10 departments, shows a more modest increase of 5.5% in 2020 over 2019 (see TableIA.IIIAin theInternet Appendix), a rate that is in line with the normal annual growth during the past two decades. It is also worth noting that a recent survey by Myers et al. (2020) reports mean effects for a sample of 4,500 scientists that are similar to ours in that the majority of respondents report sharp declines in research time.
Third, COVID-19 itself acts a natural experiment for many settings and has attracted a lot of research. Such research has more urgency than usual and might push researchers to post on SSRN (and other open-source repositories) at an earlier stage. Fourth, outside of such “topical” research, SSRN postings might capture a part of the research process that has suffered less from the crisis (e.g., later-stage work on revisions or very new projects). This would be expected if the time required to prepare an initial draft of an already up-and-running project is less than the investment required to start a new project or revise existing work for publication.
Finally, it could also be the case that survey respondents are not representative of the profession but rather are those most negatively affected by the pandemic. It is generally true that subjects that are expected to be more affected by policies coming out of the survey are more likely to participate (Bertrand and Duflo (2016)). Importantly, even if this were the case, it would not drive the cross-sectional variation (i.e., role of family structure, seniority, and gender) or the impact of mechanisms (feedback, isolation, and health) that we observe in the data. Indeed, these sources of variation, rather than the mean effect, are the main focus of the paper. To the extent that Cui, Ding, and Zhu (2021) and Kruger, Maturana, and Nickerson (2020) find cross-sectional patterns, such as gender differences in output, our findings are broadly consistent with theirs.7
The variable that captures changes in time spent on specific activities (Time_Activity) is based on a Likert scale from 1 = much less time to 5 = much more time during compared to before the pandemic. The stay-at-home measures to help curb the spread of COVID-19 thrust faculty into rapid transitions to online teaching. At the same time, many faculty took on at-home tasks, such as childcare, home schooling, and domestic chores, part of which may have been outsourced under normal circumstances. This forced researchers to reallocate their time significantly. Faculty in our sample report spending significantly less time on research, leisure, and sleep while spending more time on teaching, childcare/schooling, and household chores than before the pandemic. The effects of COVID-19 on time allocation are similar for senior and junior faculty (none of the differences between the two groups are statistically significant). In contrast to faculty, however, PhD students do not report a significant decline in time allocated to research or teaching, and they even sleep more during the pandemic. Compared to faculty, students also report a smaller increase in time devoted to childcare and schooling and a smaller decline in leisure, mostly due to the fact that PhD students are significantly less likely to have children.
The time allocation responses help with the overall interpretation of the responses to “How did the COVID-19 pandemic affect your research productivity?” Although we believe that the typical respondent equates research productivity with output (which is commonly used to evaluate performance in the profession), it is also possible that respondents express productivity in terms of output per unit time spent on research. To examine this possibility, we calculate the correlation between responses to the questions that ask about changes in research productivity and changes in time spent on research. Consistent with the output-based interpretation, we find a strong correlation (coefficient of 77% for the faculty sample) between self-reported changes in productivity and changes in time allocated to research. In TableIA.IVAin theInternet Appendix, we cross tabulate responses to these questions in a 2 × 2 matrix. The majority of respondents report identical answers to these two questions (53%, or 391 of 731). Fewer than 10% (72 of 731) give answers that are more than one level off the diagonal. If productivity were interpreted as output per hour, such a high correlation between inputs (time) and the ratio of outputs to inputs (output per unit time) would imply a research production function with increasing returns to scale that is too dramatic to be plausible. We therefore interpret responses to research productivity in terms of research output in the rest of the study.
About 30% of the faculty respondents in our final sample are female (Female). This fraction is higher among junior faculty (33.4%) and even higher among students (45.5%) than among senior faculty (27.1%). The lower representation of women among the most senior faculty is consistent with the faculty population in the economics and related fields. For example, the Committee on the Status of Women in the Economics Profession's (CSWEP) 2019 annual report indicates that women comprise 14.5% of full professors, 25.8% of associate professors, 30.3% of assistant professors, and 32.2% of graduating PhD students. The percentage of female finance faculty respondents to our survey is larger than some of the demographic census values that appear in current literature and suggests greater response rates from women.8Oversampling of women should not impact the estimated gender effects if the males and females who do respond do not have pandemic-period experiences that differ, along gender lines, from those who do not.
Family structure is a variable of interest in any analysis of how the COVID-19 pandemic affects researchers. The need to spend additional time on childcare and schooling as well as on domestic chores is likely to depend on the size of the household, the age of the dependents, and whether those tasks can be shared with partners. We employ several variables to capture these aspects of household characteristics. In light of evidence from other fields that the presence of young children reduces productivity (e.g., Krukowski, Jagsi, and Cardel (2021), Myers et al. (2020)), we focus on the ages of children (if any) and whether a respondent has a nonworking spouse or partner (Nonwork_spouse). On average 24.9% of our faculty respondents have a nonworking spouse, with senior faculty more likely to have a nonworking spouse than either junior faculty or students.
In terms of children, 62.4% of senior faculty and 55.3% of junior faculty have children between 0 and 18 years old, while only 19.9% of students have children. The age distribution of children varies predictably for senior and junior faculty. Among all respondents, 32.6% have children between 0 and 5 years old, and another 30.2% have children between 6 and 12 years old. Junior faculty tend to have younger children than senior faculty. To facilitate interpretation of our results, we construct a cardinal variable that ranges between 0 and 3 to summarize the ages of children (Childcardinal) and the corresponding level of parental care required. It is constructed as follows: 0 for no children or children 18 years old and above; 1 for children between 13 and 18 years old; 2 for children between 6 and 12 years old; and 3 for children between 0 and 5 years old.
The pandemic has caused many people to shelter at home, which has reduced the ability of faculty to interact, both professionally and socially. This lack of social connection can lead to a reduction in the ability to receive feedback on their work, feelings of social isolation, and concerns about one's physical or mental health. All such factors might affect research productivity. We construct three variables to measure these dimensions of the pandemic experience:Feedback,Isolation, andHealth.
We ask survey participants about the impact of COVID-19 on their ability to receive timely feedback (Feedback) on their research projects, working papers, or other work. We again use a Likert scale from 1 = strong positive impact to 5 = strong negative impact, so that a higher value corresponds to a more negative outcome.9On average, respondents report a negative impact on the ability to receive timely feedback. This effect is particularly pronounced among junior faculty (a difference of 0.33 compared to senior faculty,t= 5.26). In contrast, students appear somewhat better off than junior faculty in terms of getting timely feedback (a difference of 0.36,t= 4.97), perhaps because students do not require as much external research feedback yet and they have supervisors in place to provide feedback.
We also ask respondents about their well-being in the wake of COVID-19. For the purpose of this study, we focus on two concerns: isolation due to working from home (Isolation) and physical and mental health (Health). These variables are reported on a Likert scale from 1 = strongly disagree to 5 = strongly agree, where a higher value corresponds to a more negative outcome. We find widespread self-reported concerns among faculty and students about isolation and health. The means for all groups are significantly above the neutral value of three. Comparing senior and junior faculty, we find that junior faculty worry more about isolation and health than their senior colleagues (differences of 0.23,t= 2.75, and 0.45,t= 5.53, respectively). Students have similar concerns about isolation as junior faculty, but students reveal even greater concerns about physical and mental health (a difference of 0.26,t= 3.30). This finding is consistent with Woolston (2020), who reports a doubling of signs of depression among graduate students during the pandemic based on a survey at nine leading research universities in the United States.
Apart from personal characteristics, university policies and institutional resilience also impact faculty members’ personal experiences. To investigate institutional effects, we focus on two variables:ExtendClockandEmployer_Finances. TheExtendClockvariable is based on responses to a number of questions about how institutional policy has changed in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. For ease of interpretation, we defineExtendClockto be a dummy variable equal to 1 if the institution gave the option to extend the tenure clock or automatically extended the tenure clock, and to 0 otherwise. On average, 68.9% of faculty respondents say their institutions have policies that enable junior faculty to extend the tenure clock.
Similar to the concerns about isolation or physical/mental health, we ask respondents if they are worried about their employer's finances on a Likert scale from 1 = strongly disagree to 5 = strongly agree, with a higher value corresponding to a less ideal state. We use responses to this question to identify institutions that are likely to be more affected financially by the pandemic. The idea is that variation in responses to this question can capture variation in universities’ financial resilience to the crisis (e.g., endowment fluctuations, exposure to international students, etc.). Over half (53.4%) of the faculty report some concerns about employer finances. Although the mean level of concern for senior faculty is slightly larger in magnitude than for the other groups, the average value ofEmployer_Financesis not statistically different across senior faculty, junior faculty, and students.
A burgeoning literature (e.g., Schiebinger, Henderson, and Gilmartin (2008), Minello (2020)) on the impact of COVID-19 on working professionals has naturally identified the role played by family structure. Most importantly, the pandemic kept at home children who otherwise would be in school or day care during most working hours. To set the stage for more analytical empirical testing, TableIIreports descriptive statistics on the key family structure variables by gender and rank.
Among all faculty, women are significantly (at the 1% level) less likely to have a nonworking spouse than men (14.2% vs. 29.5%, as shown in Panel A). This result is consistent with survey evidence in Schiebinger, Henderson, and Gilmartin (2008), who report that male academics are more likely to have a stay-at-home partner than female academics. Panels B through D show that the difference is significant across all ranks and is increasing with seniority, from 9.9% among PhD students to 16.1% among senior faculty. We do not observe significant differences in the presence of children when we examine the full sample of faculty. However, when we decompose the sample according to faculty rank, gender differences become more obvious. TableII, Panel B, shows that senior women are somewhat more likely than men to have children between 0 and 18 years old, but the difference is only statistically significant for children between 6 and 12 years old. In contrast, Panel C shows that junior women are much less likely to have children at all age groups except children above 13. Children in this age range were likely born prior to the commencement of the respondent's academic career (perhaps even before entering the PhD program). The fact that senior women are significantly more likely than men to have young children and junior women are less likely to have children confirms the documented delay in childbirth among female scholars (e.g., Mason and Goulden (2002), Armenti (2004), Antecol, Bedard, and Stearns (2018)). There are no notable differences among PhD students.
article.II. Empirical Results:
COVID-19 disrupted normal life and forced people to change how they allocate their time. To characterize the effect on researchers, we ask respondents to report changes in their time allocation compared to before the start of the pandemic across six categories of activities. We then analyze how the changes in time allocation vary with gender and family structure. The six categories included in our analysis are time spent on: research (Time_Research), teaching (Time_Teaching), domestic chores (Time_Chores), childcare (Time_Childcare), leisure activities (Time_Leisure), and sleep (Time_Sleep).
The coefficients in equation (1) are log odds ratios in an ordered logit model and carry a subscripti, as they are equation specific. In addition to equation (1), we explore the role of the interaction betweenFemaleandNonwork_spouseas well as the triple interaction of the explanatory variables. These extra interactions do not have sufficient power to identify statistically significant time allocation patterns for the time allocation or the research patterns in subsequent tables, and thus we omit them and resort to the parsimonious model with variables that are most central to our interest. TableIIIreports the results.
The qualitative interpretation of the coefficients in TableIIIis similar to a linear regression model in that a positive (negative) coefficient suggests a correlation in the same sign between the covariate and the dependent variable, holding other covariates constant. The same is true for interpretation of the statistical significance.
TableIIIshows striking gender effects in how the pandemic has changed researchers’ time allocation. The estimated coefficients onFemaleimplies that women allocate significantly (at the 5% level) less time to research and significantly (at the 1% level) more time to teaching relative to men following the onset of the pandemic. The magnitudes of these two coefficients are comparable, and thus changes in time allocated to all other categories exhibit little differences along the gender dimension. A nonworking spouse/partner (Nonwork_spouse), as expected, decreases time allocated in childcare (significant at the 10% level), but most of the time saved appears to go toward teaching (significant at the 10% level). On balance, and somewhat surprisingly, the research time advantage of researchers with a nonworking spouse is not statistically significant.
The presence of children in the family, especially young children (Childcardinal), significantly (at the 1% level) increases time spent on childcare and domestic chores. This extra time has to be diverted from other activities, resulting in reduced allocations to research, sleep, and leisure activities, in that order. All of these negative effects are significant at the 1% level. The coefficients on the interaction termFemale×Childcardinalindicate that the presence of children, especially young children, has an incremental impact on female researchers such that they spend even more time on childcare and even less time on leisure activities than their male counterparts. Both effects are significant at the 5% level. The estimated coefficient on theFemale×Childcardinalinteraction term in theTime_Researchregression is insignificant, implying that women with children strive to maintain roughly the same research time compared to men with a similar family structure.
The quantitative magnitudes of the coefficients in an ordered logit model are not as straightforward as those in a linear regression. Here the coefficients are log odds ratios, and thus the coefficients exponentiated become the odds ratio. As an example, consider the coefficient of −0.420 onFemalein the equation forTime_Research. Suppose the probability that the time allocated to research has changed in a particular way, such as “much less time” (level 1 of 5), is given by (1 –p). Then the odds ratio of the researcher reporting a level higher than the current one isp/(1– p). The value of the coefficient suggests that, if we change researcher gender from male to female while keeping the other variables constant, the odds ratio that the researcher could go to a higher level of time allocated to research is reduced by 34.3% (exp(−0.42) = 0.657; given the neutral value is equal to 1, 0.657 is 34.3% below the reference level).10
A more intuitive way to present the gender disparity result in TableIIIfrom the ordered logit model is to show the predicted probabilities of a subject ending up in each of five levels (from “much less time” to “much more time”) separately for men and women, assuming that the two groups have the same family structure as imputed by the sample average values ofNonwork_spouseandChildcardinal. Figure1, which contains six panels, compares the predicted probabilities that an average woman and an average man would respond with one of the five levels of time allocation for each of the six time categories (with the other covariates held at their sample averages). ForTime_Research(top left panel), women lead men in reporting “much less” (24.3% vs. 18.5%) and “less” (41.7% vs. 39.3%) time spent in research compared to prepandemic levels, and trail men at all three higher levels. With respect to time spent teaching (top middle panel), women are less likely to report the three lowest levels (1.0%, 6.2%, and 26.6% for “much less,” “less,” and “about the same” respectively) than men (1.4%, 8.6%, and 32.2%), and more likely to report the “more” and “much more” levels. For domestic chores (top right panel), very few men and women report less work than before, but women are less likely to remain “about the same” than men (33.2% vs. 36.8%) and correspondingly are more represented in the two higher levels. A very similar pattern applies to time allocated to childcare (bottom left panel). For leisure activities (bottom middle panel), proportionally more women report “much less” than men (37.3% vs. 32.8%). Finally, the bottom right panel shows that proportionally fewer women report the same amount of sleep time (57.1% vs. 59.3%), with the difference mostly reflected in the “less time” category.
We apply the same methodology to show the predicted probabilities of being in any of the five reported time allocation categories in terms of childcare demands using the presence of children and their ages: no child at home, children 13 to 18 years old, children 6 to 12 years old, and children 0 to 5 years old. As can be seen in the top left panel of Figure2, the probabilities of answering “much less time” allocated to research increases monotonically with childcare demands: 10.9%, 17.8%, 28.1%, and 41.1%, respectively. The pattern for the “less time” category is similar but less steep. The three higher level categories show a reverse ordering. For example, the predicted probabilities for reporting “more time” in research are 26.2%, 17.8%, 11.1%, and 6.6%. The steepest contrast in this sorting, not surprisingly, occurs in time allocation to childcare (bottom left panel). The predicted probabilities of reporting “much more time” are 2.8%, 14.4%, 49.8%, and 85.3% from no child at home to at least one child in the 0 to 5 age range. A similar but less steep pattern obtains for time spent in domestic chores, and reverse patterns are observed for time spent in leisure and sleep. Interestingly, there is little difference in time spent teaching as we vary childcare demands. This result could reflect the fact that time required for teaching is less flexible than research time and has to be put in regardless of other constraints. Indeed, with the move to online classes, the pandemic may have imposed a uniform learning time cost on all.
We note that the ultimate effects of a nonworking spouse and children on male and female researchers is more nuanced than what the regressions and the corresponding figures indicate. This is because the coefficients represent estimates conditional on a particular state, but they ignore the differential unconditional probabilities of being in the state for men and women. The summary statistics in TableIIshow that a nonworking spouse is much more common for men, while junior women are less likely to have children. Hence, the total impact of COVID-19 will vary even if the coefficients on these variables are similar across different groups.
The time allocation changes reported in TableIIIcapture trade-offs for the scarce resource of time. Although research time is a prerequisite for research output, it is one of many inputs into research productivity. To understand how the pandemic has affected research productivity as an output, we turn to our output measure of self-assessed research productivity. We first look at how research productivity varies according to predetermined variables, namely, gender and family structure. We next add time inputs (time spent on nonresearch activities), which capture how researchers are allocating time differently during the pandemic. We then layer in variables that capture concerns regarding social interactions and health, which may also affect people differentially. Mental health is of particular concern, given broad evidence that mental health affects employment prospects and workplace productivity (Bubonya, Cobb-Clark, and Wooden (2017), Frijters, Johnston, and Shields (2014)) and that the associated costs are large in magnitude (Greenberg et al. (2015)). Our analysis provides the first (to our knowledge) evidence of potential linkages between research productivity in academia to pandemic-related increases in concerns about isolation and mental health.
We begin by estimating an ordered logistic regression analogous to the time allocation estimations of TableIII, but here the dependent variable is the effect of the pandemic on research productivity, ranging from 1 (strong negative effect) to 5 (strong positive effect). The results are reported in TableIV. In TableV, we run the same regressions as in TableIVafter separating subjects into three groups according to seniority—senior (i.e., tenured) scholars, junior scholars, and PhD students. This analysis allows us to examine potentially differential impacts of COVID-19 at different stages of a researcher's career.
Column (1) of TableIVand columns (1), (3), and (5) of TableVinclude as regressors only exogenous factors, including the gender and family structure variables. TableIV, column (1), shows that having a nonworking spouse helps research productivity (at the 10% significance level). The breakdown of subjects into three groups based on seniority rank in TableVreveals that the effect of a nonworking spouse is driven by junior faculty, not tenured faculty or PhD students. Young children negatively impact research productivity during the pandemic for all faculty. The latter effect is highly significant (at the 1% level) for senior and junior faculty, but not significant for PhD students. Even after controlling for family structure, however, women are still significantly (at the 1% level) more negatively impacted by COVID-19. Moreover, the magnitude of the gender effect increases as faculty become more senior, going from small and insignificant for PhD students to sizable and marginally significant (at the 10% level) for junior faculty to large and highly significant (at the 1% level) for senior faculty.
We can think of two possible explanations for the gender disparity in research productivity even after controlling for family structure. First, senior women generally take on disproportionately more service work for both their employers and the profession due to their underrepresentation. For example, Guarino and Borden (2017) document that women, especially senior women, shoulder disproportionately more service work such as serving on committees and mentoring students. The volume of such work ballooned during the pandemic as most schools set up many task forces to address the challenges created by the pandemic. As such, female scholars, especially senior women, in the profession may have faced disproportionately more administrative “taxes” on their research productivity. Second, the impact of childcare transmits via the coauthor network. A researcher's productivity could be negatively impacted if their coauthors are time-constrained due to increased childcare needs. TableIIIshows that women allocate more time to childcare than men even given the same family structure, which leads to a larger indirect effect on women as a whole (including those without young children) because women in finance have significantly more female coauthors as documented by Sherman and Tookes (2020). Sarsons et al. (2021) estimate that women in economics, on average, are three times more likely to have female coauthors than men.
Similar to Figures1and2, Figure3presents the predicted probabilities of changes in research productivity based on the model of TableIV, column (1). The results echo those of the time allocation graphs summarized in Figures1and2. In Figure3, the left panel shows a sizable gender gap in the predicted reporting of “much less” research productivity, at 53.9% for women and 43.0% for men. The right panel shows the impact of children. Consistent with the patterns in Figure2, the impact of children is steepest in the “much less” category. The predicted probability of a researcher feeling “much less” productive than before is 31.8% for those without children at home, 42.7% for those with children 13 to 18 years old, 55.4% for those with children 6 to 12 years old; and 67.4% for those with children 0 to 5 years old. An equally steep distribution obtains for the predicted probabilities that researchers will report “more” or “much more” productivity ranging from 5.8% for faculty with children 0 to 5 years old to 21.6% for faculty with no children. These results highlight positive productivity shocks for a segment of the faculty population that could be contributing to the increased flow of papers posting in repositories or submitted to journals during the pandemic.
In columns (2) and (3) of TableIV, we further explore potential mechanisms underlying the relationship between research productivity and both gender and family structure, including the role played by the time inputs. For this analysis, we also include the time allocation variables to assess their effect on the predetermined family structure and gender variables. Column (2) shows that the negative effect on research associated with having young children is completely explained by time allocated to childcare and chores. Column (3) shows an additional negative effect on research associated with time devoted to teaching. These effects all significantly reduce research productivity (at the 1% level).
In the last column of TableIV, we add three variables that capture the professional environment and social-psychological state of faculty. In particular, the three variablesFeedback,Isolation, andHealthmeasure concerns about timely feedback on research, isolation, and physical/mental health on a Likert scale ranging from 1 = not concerned to 5 = very concerned. Column (4) of TableIVshows that a perceived lack of feedback on research and concerns for health are highly significantly (at the 1% level) correlated with research productivity; the feeling of isolation also contributes negatively (at the 10% level) to research productivity, albeit less significantly.
Returning to TableV, the breakdown by seniority rank further reveals that the time consumed by childcare affects junior scholars more than seniors (both significant at the 1% level), but does not significantly impact PhD students (who are also less likely to have children). The impact of time allocated to teaching affects faculty but not PhD students (perhaps because their teaching is limited or confined to supporting roles). Domestic chores affect all groups roughly evenly. Lack of feedback on research and concern for personal health affects self-reported research productivity of all groups similarly, but perceived isolation hits PhD students the hardest. Students are the only group with a significant effect of isolation, which is consistent with the increased rates of depression and anxiety among graduate students reported in Woolston (2020).
Several interactive effects are worth noting. First, comparing the different specifications in TablesIVandVsheds light on the extent to which time choices absorb the effect of family structure. The time choices only slightly offset the effect of a nonworking spouse. However, the inclusion of changes in time allocated to childcare, chores, and teaching render the coefficients onChildcardinalinsignificant, both economically and statistically, for all groups of subjects. Such absorption implies that, in this context, the variableChildcardinalis not picking up heterogeneity between households with and without children outside of the parental time commitment to childcare during a time when the usual help (day care, babysitters, housekeepers, etc.) is not available. The contrast between the baseline and extended regressions in TablesIVandVsuggests that the impact of family structure on research productivity during the COVID-19 pandemic was more likely to be causal than an outcome of unobserved heterogeneity across households that is correlated with children.
Second, the coefficient on the interaction termFemale×Childcardinalis positive and significant (at the 1% level) after time allocation is accounted for (TableIV, columns (3) and (4)). When we look at the results by rank, we find that this remains true for the faculty subsample (TableV, columns (2) and (4)), but not PhD students (TableV, column (6)). Moreover, the inclusion of the interaction term does not notably change the magnitude of the coefficients onFemale. Therefore, women seem to be more effective at offsetting the loss of research time due to time spent with children in a more research-productive way than men. This is a novel finding and is consistent with the idea that women may have already learned how to multitask and to manage work-life balance to a greater extent than men.
Finally, the inclusion of social psychological variables reduces the gender effect: the coefficient onFemaleincreases from – 0.718 to −0.514 from columns (3) to (4) in TableIV, suggesting that part of the gender disparity might be driven by differential impacts of COVID-19 on the environment and psychology of women and men. In the next section, we explore this mechanism in more detail.
In equation (2), the dependent variable,Mech, is one of the variables representing potential mechanisms through which COVID-19 impacts research productivity. The mechanisms that we consider include concerns about feedback, isolation, and physical/mental health (Feedback,Isolation, andHealth). We also include the family structure variables,Nonworking_spouseandChildcardinal. The independent variables include gender, seniority rank dummy variables for junior faculty and students, and the interaction between the two. We highlight junior faculty and PhD students because their long-term career outcomes are potentially more vulnerable to the pandemic shock. In these regressions, senior faculty serve as the omitted category. TableVIreports the results.
In TableVI, Panel A reports results for the faculty subsample only; the regressions in Panel B include both faculty and PhD students. Overall, the results in Panel A of TableVIshow that junior faculty are in a significantly weaker position to withstand the COVID-19 shock compared to senior faculty: they are more likely to express concerns about lack of feedback on their research (column (1)), to feel isolated (column (2)), and to worry about their own physical or mental health (column (3)). The last two columns examine the distribution of pre-existing family structure in relation to junior faculty and student status. The results confirm that junior scholars and PhD students are less likely to have the help of a nonworking spouse and are more likely to have young children (compared to the omitted category of senior scholars). All of these factors, significant at the 5% level or better, have a negative effect on research productivity, contributing to the overall lower research productivity reported by junior researchers in TableI. In sum, despite the fact that the elasticities with which mechanisms affecting productivity impact junior and senior faculty are similar (TableV, columns (2), (4), and (6)), the intensity of the mechanisms is consistently greater for junior faculty.
Focusing on gender, we find that women are also more concerned about feedback than their male faculty peers (significant at the 1% level), possibly due to the relatively more narrow professional network and scarcity of other senior female scholars in the field.11The network issue has only been exacerbated during the COVID-19 pandemic, as conferences and seminars went virtual and other opportunities to interact with colleagues were dramatically reduced. Although women express similar feelings of increased isolation as men, they express more concerns about their own health (significant at the 5% level) than men. They are also less likely to have a nonworking spouse though the presence of children does not differ significantly. In short, among faculty, the mechanisms affecting research productivity tend to be more intense for women than men.
Several interaction terms reveal additional nuances. For example, the reliably negative interaction ofJunior×Femalein theFeedbackregression (column (1)) indicates that concerns regarding feedback are similar for male and female junior faculty (and significantly higher than for senior faculty), but are significantly stronger for senior women compared to senior men. Analogously, the reliably negative coefficient on theJunior×Femaleinteraction term in the regression in whichChildcardinalis the dependent variable (column (5)) suggests that junior men are more likely than junior women to have high childcare needs (consistent with TableII, which shows that junior women are less likely than junior men to have children).
TableVI, Panel B, extends the analyses to include students. Although students do not express increased concerns about feedback, perhaps because many are not at the stage of having a project that requires feedback beyond their supervisors, they do report significantly heightened concerns about isolation (at the 5% level) and health (at the 10% level) compared to faculty. Due to the stage in their lives in which many students attend graduate school, students are less likely to have a nonworking spouse and are less likely to have children. Connecting results in TableVIwith those in TableV, we find that not only are PhD students more likely to feel isolated and to express concerns about their health, but also that for a given level of such concerns, these factors exert a stronger effect on research productivity. Moreover, the coefficients on the interaction betweenFemaleandStudentin TableVIconsistently point to a negative impact on research productivity, although only the effect on health is significant (at the 5% level). In other words, female PhD students are significantly more likely to be concerned about their own health compared to their male classmates. The combined results from the student subsample call for greater awareness by institutions and the profession about the physical and social effects of a negative shock such as the COVID-19 lockdowns on the next generation of scholars.
TableVshows that time allocation, feedback, isolation, and health mechanisms all contribute to the average decline in research productivity for faculty and PhD students alike. The economic magnitudes of these effects, in addition to the directional effects, are of considerable interest. To calibrate the economic significance, we calculate how a particular mechanism affects the probability of a researcher being “much less” research productive, the most negative impact category. We focus on two categories of mechanisms—time mechanisms and production mechanisms. For “time spent on childcare,” for example, we are interested in how researchers who have an input value of 5 (“much more” time spent on childcare) fare relative to those who have an input value of 3 (“no change” in time spent on childcare), in terms of being “much less” research productive. We estimate this effect as a marginal effect in ordered logit estimation, setting all other variables at their respective mean values. Analogous calculations are performed for all of the other variables. We depict these marginal effects in Figure4.
In interpreting marginal effects in Likert scales for time allocation, it is useful to ask whether respondents map the Likert scale to hours (where the neutral value of 3 corresponds to zero hours of change) and whether, across categories of activities, respondents account for the 24-hour budget constraint each day. One way to check this is to examine whether individual responses average to 3 (neutral) across time-use categories. Alternatively, respondents could report percentage changes in time, in which case the adding-up constraint would not be evident in the data. In TableIA.VAin theInternet Appendix, we show the mean and median response across all activities. The median is precisely 3, and the mean is very close to 3, at 3.06. Although the 24-hour budget constraint does not map neatly to a Likert scale, the survey data suggest that many respondents treated time allocation changes on the survey scale of 1 to 5 as a zero-sum change (i.e., in units of time rather than in percentage changes). We therefore use the budget constraint interpretation.
In Figure4, Panel A, the marginal effects of time spent on teaching, childcare, and chores are all large. For example, senior faculty spending “much more” time on childcare, relative to “no change,” are 54.7 percentage points more likely to be “much less” research productive. The childcare effect is somewhat lower for junior faculty and dramatically lower for students. We observe a similar pattern for time spent on chores across ranks, with those devoting “much more” time to chores again over 50 percentage points more likely to be “much less” research productive. These effects of work-life balances on research output during this unusual time, while perhaps temporary, could have long-lasting effects on career outcomes and competitive positioning across demographics.
The other balances that researchers have to make are work-work balances, allocating time between, say, teaching and research. In Figure4, Panel A, we depict the marginal effects of time spent on teaching on the probability of being “much less” research productive. Senior faculty who spend “much more” time teaching (relative to “no change”) experience a 39.0 percentage point higher probability of being “much less” research productive. This effect is one-third lower for junior faculty, perhaps reflecting their additional pressure to perform in research and/or the extra burdens that senior faculty experience in teaching as they are more likely to teach multiple subjects and to lead the curriculum.
We turn to the other production mechanisms—the well-being and social connection variables—in Figure4, Panel B. These mechanisms likely affect productivity per unit of time and thus can contribute to reductions in research independent of time allocations. The figure shows effects that rival those of the time allocation variables—note that the figures are on the same y-axis scale. Senior faculty, junior faculty, and students who express the most significant worries about feedback are 49.9, 41.7, and 33.7 percentage points more likely to report being “much less” research productive. All of these magnitudes are large. Feedback is clearly a critical input in our research production process. The lack of feedback may be due to a reduction in feedback from local colleagues (especially, perhaps for students and juniors), but the lack of conversations at in-person conferences and seminars is also clearly taking a toll on our profession's ability to exchange ideas and provide feedback. Even the emergence of seminars and conferences on Zoom does not fully restore spontaneous interactions and serendipitous brainstorming, and this shortfall may be particularly hard on scholars who have yet to establish themselves in the profession.
Isolation has the smallest effect and varies relatively little by rank, but we nevertheless draw attention to the effect of isolation on students. Students who are “much more” worried about isolation are 23.4 percentage points more likely to be “much less” research productive. Finally, we turn to physical and mental health. Health concerns have a dramatic effect on research productivity. Seniors who are much more worried about health are 46.9 percentage points more likely to be “much less” research productive. This effect drops to 30.3 percentage points and 25.0 percentage points for students, presumably because younger people are physically more robust in general. However, it is common to hear stories of many PhD students struggling with mental as well as physical health during the pandemic.
We note that the marginal effects across groups do not account for the fact that the groups exhibit dispersion in their inclusion in a given category (say, “much more” worried about health). This leads us to analyze not just the marginal impact of each mechanism, but also the expected total impact for anyone in a given rank within the population. Put differently, while the marginal effects shown in Figure4summarize the impact for a given change in a particular mechanism by rank, they do not account for the differential probability of the mechanism across ranks. For instance, although time spent on childcare has a bigger effect on senior faculty, junior faculty are more likely to have small children at home. Thus, the expected effect of childcare on junior faculty could still be greater because of their greater likelihood of having children in the first place. To make more pertinent comparisons of the relative magnitudes of the effects that account for both the marginal effect and the exposure of a particular group to the mechanism, we plot the expected marginal effect on research productivity in each population by multiplying the marginal effects shown in Figure4by the probability that each group reports “much more” (i.e., a value of 5) for the input of interest. We summarize the results in Figure5.
In Figure5, Panel A, we summarize the time variables. In stark contrast to the marginal effects, we find that junior faculty are more likely to endure a negative effect from childcare and chores. For example, a junior faculty member is 17.8 percentage points more likely to report a significant negative research impact that can be traced to a significant increase in childcare responsibilities despite the fact that the marginal effect of both variables is smaller for junior than senior faculty. This result obtains because junior faculty are more likely to report significant increases in time spent on childcare due to the stage of the life cycle, they are in. In contrast, the expected marginal effects on students are quite small because they are less likely to have significant increases in childcare and chores during the pandemic and thus the marginal effects of these demands are also smaller.
In Figure5, Panel B, we summarize the well-being and social connection variables. These patterns are also starkly different from those reported in Figure4, Panel B. Junior faculty are much more likely to be affected by the lack of feedback than senior faculty or students. The expected increase in the probability that a representative junior faculty is “much less” research productive because of being “much more” worried about feedback is 16.2 percentage points. The effect on students is now materially lower than that on junior faculty because students are less likely to be in the category associated with the highest worry about feedback. This emphasis on feedback for junior researchers in particular may reflect a setting in which students’ advisors continue to look after students advanced in their dissertation work, but juniors lack such a built-in feedback process.
In contrast, worries about both isolation and health have larger expected impacts for the student population than for faculty. The magnitudes are important in many ways. We find that any student is 10.5 percentage points more likely to be “much less” research productive because of being “much more” worried about physical and mental health. The effect is also large for junior faculty. This finding suggests that 10% of students (and a little less for junior faculty) are suffering severe research production declines because of mental and physical health concerns.
Bringing some of these findings together, we find that junior faculty are most affected by the lack of feedback and childcare burdens. Students are most affected by concerns about feedback, isolation, and health. Institutions’ efforts to support their faculty and PhD student populations should thus account for the heterogeneous ways in which the crisis has taken a toll on research.
article.III. Institutional Variation and Respons:
In this section, we explore variation in the effect of COVID-19 on research productivity across institutions. We focus on differences in financial strength and the tenure accommodation policies introduced as a result of the pandemic.
Research requires resources that are affected by the institution's financial situation. Not only do universities differ widely in terms of their endowments, revenues, and gifts, but they have also been affected by the COVID-19 shock differently. For example, schools that rely more heavily on tuition from international students have been hit harder (Fischer (2019)). We attempt to capture faculty members’ assessments of their institutions’ financial health by using the responses to a question about employer finances. Specifically, we use responses to “I worry about the finances of my employer,” where responses are measured on a five-point Likert scale from 1 = strongly disagree to 5 = strongly agree.
To quantify the impact of employer finances on employee research productivity, we estimate an ordered logistic regression in which the dependent variable isResearch_Productivity.
